insights.name: Prove the Number
insights.rule: If the line states a quantified result (weight, steps, dollars, time), cut to the screen, scale, receipt, or check displaying that exact figure while the line lands.
insights.why: Abstract claims are forgettable; visual proof converts the promise into concrete evidence, forcing the viewer to trust the premise immediately.

insights.name: Show the Named Object
insights.rule: The instant the line names a specific object (shoes, slime, device, app), center a close-up of that object in frame; never let the audio outrun the visual.
insights.why: Locking audio and visual onto the same subject eliminates confusion and satisfies the brain’s immediate need to identify the topic.

insights.name: Contradict the Audio
insights.rule: If the line states an expectation or brag ("it’s easy," "turned out great"), show visual evidence of the opposite (a failed timer, a hostile environment, a pained expression).
insights.why: Cognitive dissonance between what is heard and seen creates an open loop that the viewer must close by continuing to watch.

insights.name: Start Moving Before Explaining
insights.rule: Begin with a body in motion (exiting a car, pulling up shorts, walking away) while the voiceover opens with context like "here's how" or "let me explain."
insights.why: Movement captures peripheral attention before the ear processes language; the viewer stays to sync the action with the explanation.

insights.name: Pair the Mundane with the Extreme
insights.rule: Match a philosophical, scientific, or shocking statement with an ordinary, everyday visual (opening a door, walking down steps, stepping over a curb).
insights.why: The mismatch generates mystery; viewers cannot resolve why a radical claim is paired with a boring image, so they keep watching.

insights.name: Reveal the Aftermath First
insights.rule: Open on the result or emotional aftermath (sweaty face, silhouette by a car, walking away with weights) while the line admits failure or claims an impossible feat.
insights.why: Showing the effect before the cause creates a curiosity gap; the audience needs the backstory to explain the visual state.

insights.name: Layer Text for Reframe
insights.rule: Overlay text that re-frames or intensifies the spoken line (e.g., a timer with "Extremely painful" while the line says "easy," or "Banned" over a shoe).
insights.why: Dual-channel processing (reading + listening) deepens engagement; text can add irony or stakes that audio alone cannot deliver quickly.

insights.name: Introduce the Face Before the Stunt
insights.rule: When announcing an extreme challenge, show the speaker looking at the camera or standing in the environment before showing the action.
insights.why: Viewers invest in people, not tasks; establishing the human stake creates parasocial buy-in that outlasts pure spectacle.

insights.name: Split Screen for Scale
insights.rule: When the line describes intensity, complexity, or multiple actors, use a split frame to show simultaneous action rather than sequential cuts.
insights.why: A split frame visually proves the claim of intensity; it packs more information into the same seconds, raising perceived value.

insights.name: Use Environment as Counterpoint
insights.rule: Place the subject in a setting that clashes with the audio goal (snowy street for making a drink, beach for qualifying, sun silhouette for failure).
insights.why: Environmental contradiction signals that the story defies normal logic, suggesting the outcome will be unexpected.

insights.name: Tighten on the Stakes
insights.rule: When the line includes a price, ban, or rare label, cut to a tight close-up of the object with overlaid text reinforcing the superlative.
insights.why: Close-ups increase perceived value and urgency; they signal that this object is the entire reason for the video.

insights.name: Show the Cast for Social Stakes
insights.rule: If the line involves other people (friends, competitors, a crush), show the group or the other person in the frame immediately.
insights.why: Social dynamics are inherently sticky; introducing the cast turns an individual claim into a relational drama.

insights.name: Frame Physical Vulnerability
insights.rule: When the line admits failure, pain, or exhaustion, show the body in that exact state (sweaty skin, heavy breathing, walking away defeated).
insights.why: Authentic physical states trigger empathy; viewers mirror the stress and need to know what caused it.

insights.name: Show Preparation to Validate the Promise
insights.rule: While teasing a result or full video, show the preparatory action (putting on gear, stepping over a threshold, holding a remote) rather than a static pose.
insights.why: Preparation signals the promise is already in motion, transforming a hypothetical hook into an active narrative.

insights.name: Use Intimate Framing for Emotional Reveals
insights.rule: For personal revelations (crushes, pride, failure), use mirrors, close-ups, or bedroom/bathroom settings that feel private and unguarded.
insights.why: Intimate framing creates a sense of privileged access; viewers feel they are being confided in, which strengthens retention.
